failure_modes.text: Legacy role allocation can keep work with the wrong owner even when marginal efficiency has shifted.
failure_modes.source_quote: legacy role allocation blocks higher-leverage specialization
failure_modes.mitigation: Revisit role allocation when marginal efficiency shifts; legacy assignments persist through inertia even after the comparative advantage has moved.

failure_modes.text: Over-specialization can create inflexibility and blind teams to novelty outside their habitual frame.
failure_modes.mitigation: Guard against over-specialization by maintaining minimum competence in adjacent domains so the team can recognize novelty outside its habitual frame.

failure_modes.text: Comparative advantage can be misused as an excuse to avoid building minimum adjacent competence.
failure_modes.source_quote: avoid building minimum competence in adjacent skills
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish comparative advantage from convenient avoidance: the model justifies focus, not an excuse to ignore gaps that create systemic fragility.

premortem_questions.text: Who should own this work if we reallocated by marginal efficiency instead of by tradition?
What alternative perspective outside my specialty would expose a better role split?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What alternative perspectives, outside of my area of expertise

premortem_questions.text: Which non-core skills still need baseline competence even if they are not our comparative edge?

heuristics.text: Map the zone of genius and complementary strengths before assigning ownership.
Use the Pareto principle to focus effort on the work with the highest return on talent.
Design roles so the organization becomes more than the sum of specialized parts.
heuristics.source_quote: more than the sum of its parts
